_comment: Offline retrieval hints for surface_review.py. Each entry's `triggers` are matched (case-insensitive substring) against the source under review; matched `note`s are injected into the reviewer/critic prompts as VERIFIED REFERENCE NOTES. Notes are factual protocol/CWE/CVE knowledge the local model often lacks; they are only injected when a trigger is present, and the prompt instructs the model to use a note ONLY where it matches an anchor actually in the code. Keep notes short, factual, and source-grounded. Extend per subsystem.

entries.note: CVE-2018-15688 (systemd): a remote heap buffer overflow in the DHCPv6 client arose from unsigned-underflow arithmetic in option-length / remaining-space math in the option-append path (size - offset wrapping huge, then memcpy(optlen)). The DHCPv4 dhcp_option_append here is the direct analog. CWE-787 / CWE-191. cve_analog = "CVE-2018-15688".

DHCP option parsing is a read path. Two canonical bugs: (a) if the message length is below the fixed 240-byte DHCP header (DHCP_MESSAGE_SIZE) and option space is computed as (len - 240) with unsigned math, it underflows to a huge bound -> massive out-of-bounds READ; reject short packets before subtracting. (b) Per option [code][len][data]: advancing by the 1-byte len without first checking len bytes remain over-reads the buffer. CWE-125 (read) / CWE-191 (underflow), NOT a write/CWE-787.

RFC 2131/2132 option overload (option 52): when set, the fixed sname[64] and file[128] BOOTP fields are re-parsed as additional option space. The parser must honor overload exactly once; an overloaded section that itself requests overload (or a naive recursive re-parse) causes loops / double-processing -> DoS or parser-state confusion. Needs a once-guard. CWE-674 (recursion) / CWE-400 (DoS).

DHCP FQDN option (code 81): in canonical (E-flag) form the host name is DNS wire-format -- a sequence of length-prefixed labels. A decoder must bound each label length against the remaining option length and must reject or bound DNS compression pointers (0xC0). The DHCP_MAX_FQDN_LENGTH (255) overall cap does NOT prevent a single over-long label from reading out of bounds. CWE-125.

struct iphdr is the fixed 20-byte IPv4 header ONLY when ihl == 5. If ihl > 5 (IP options present) the real UDP header and payload start AFTER those options, so a packed struct {iphdr; udphdr; dhcp;} that reads udp/dhcp at fixed offsets misparses / over-reads. A raw-socket header-verify step must reject ihl != 5 (or re-derive offsets) and validate protocol == UDP, tot_len/udp len consistency, and destination port. The culprit is the earlier variable-length IP header, never the trailing DHCP options. CWE-125.

Big-endian typed fields (be32_t/be16_t: xid, flags, addresses, magic) must be byte-swapped before use. The DHCP magic cookie (0x63825363) must be compared as the byte-swapped constant (e.g. htobe32). This magic check is frequently the ONLY pre-parse sanity gate on an unauthenticated, spoofable network packet; a missing/weak comparison, or an unswapped flags field (flipping the broadcast bit), defeats validation. CWE-697 / CWE-20.

In BOOTP/DHCP the hardware-address length 'hlen' is an 8-bit on-wire field (0..255), but 'chaddr' is a fixed 16-byte buffer. Any copy or iteration driven by hlen into/out of chaddr must clamp to 16; the 16-byte RFC limit is not enforced by the uint8_t type, so a malicious hlen overflows the buffer. CWE-120 / CWE-787.
